summary: DHH shares his 2019 reading list of roughly 20 books spanning philosophy, history, economics, climate science, and management. The list reveals a deep engagement with Erich Fromm's humanistic psychology, critical examinations of neoliberal economics, and historical accounts of societal fragility. He is particularly drawn to works that challenge mainstream narratives about progress, capitalism, and human nature. Several management and business books disappoint him as ideas that should have been blog posts rather than full-length works.
key_points: Erich Fromm emerges as DHH's most influential thinker, with five books read, praising his ability to diagnose modern materialism and critique both capitalism and communism
A strong thread of skepticism toward neoliberal economics runs through the list, with books like The Divide and Winners Take All challenging narratives about global development and elite philanthropy
DHH is impatient with modern business books that stretch a single insight to book length, dismissing both Radical Candor and The Tyranny of Metrics as ideas that should have been blog posts
Historical accounts of societal collapse (the plague, the Troubles, climate projections) reinforce a worldview that human civilization is fragile and progress is not inevitable
Snowden's memoir earns strong praise, with DHH calling for a presidential pardon and describing mass surveillance capabilities as 'almost required reading'
money_quotes: Things got the way they are through an endless series of historical accidents, unlikely events, and forks in the road. Not through some deterministic path.
An enjoyable reminder that there is little new under the sun, and that much of what we must do is to continue repackaging eternal truths for a new context.
Just the fact that HALF of all the greenhouse gasses that are warming the earth were released since Seinfeld aired on TV. That's my life time!
May a future president see the courage, wisdom, and self-sacrifice Snowden committed to and give him the pardon and the parade that he so rightly deserves.
This should have been a blog post.
Is the worker doing mindless assembly work at a factory really any better off whether the plant is owned by a capitalist or the state?
Radical Candor isn't just brutal honesty. It's emphatic honesty. Telling people where they stand from a position of care, not disinterest.
Disneyland is presented as imaginary in order to make us believe that the rest is real.
key_insight: DHH's reading reveals a consistent worldview: mainstream narratives about progress, capitalism, and management are largely self-serving myths, and genuine understanding requires engaging with thinkers who challenge comfortable assumptions.
